speaker: interviewer
text: Roll three, Renee Firestone. OK, you crossed into Czechoslovakia after having your bicycles taken away by the Russian soldiers and there you are in a restaurant in Czechoslovakia. How many of you were in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones that started out on the bicycles. And I walked over to the restaurateur and I told him that I'm from Czechoslovakia and that we are just coming out of the concentration camp. Not that he didn't know. With our shaved hands and the way we were dressed, of course he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still wearing the dress with the yellow streak in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that yellow streak stayed on forever. That's why I got rid of the dress. I wish today I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could have started a new trend in fashion.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Right. Anyway, this man was very kind. He asked me where are we going to sleep and I said to him that we still don't know. And he said, well, I should come back around 5 o'clock. He closes the restaurant at 5 o'clock. First he fed us, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he told us to come back at 5 o'clock and that he will let us sleep in the booths of the restaurant. He will lock us in. Which we did and that was our first night in real freedom. And then following day he gave us breakfast. And we were on our way and we kept marching. And later on of course we found out that there is hardly any civilian transportation, that everything was occupied by the military. And so we were living dangerously. We asked the Russian soldiers to pick us up on their trucks and take us wherever they went. We didn't care. We didn't know where we're going. We were just hoping that maybe we'll run into some prisoners who are coming out of the camp. And that was really what we were doing, wandering around Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then? How far did you go? Where did you end up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wound up the first time in Brno, Brunn. And we found out that the Red Cross has a place where we can sleep if we want to and they feed us. Until then we were sleeping in parks or in barns, whatever we found on the road and we begged for food. People recognized who we were and were fairly kind to us. Some were more human than others. And in Brunn the Red Cross also gave us a few crowns so we had a little bit of pocket money. From there we went to Slovakia, to Bratislava. There also the Red Cross took care of us for a few days. And then somebody told us that they heard that in Budapest there is a school where people register, those who come back from the war. And so we decided to go to Budapest. Didn't matter where we're going. Something very interesting happened, and this also relates to how the Russians behaved in Europe. We begged ourselves on a train and the conductor was very kind to us and permitted us to sit on top of the coal. So we were watching what was going on on the train. The trains were packed with Russian soldiers, but some of the girls coming out of the American front, the American DP camps or from the American zone, they had big bags of stuff that the Americans gave them. They were all dressed in American uniforms, they had boots on, and they had these big bags of cigarettes and chocolate and stuff. And they sat there eating in front of us and we had nothing. We had absolutely nothing. Never offered us anything. And that night we were sitting on top of the coal of course not sleeping, very excited about are we going to find anybody in Budapest or not. And in the middle of the night, we noticed that these big sacks on which these girls were sitting are getting lower and lower and lower. Towards the morning, we saw that they were sitting really on the floor. We also notice that some Russian soldiers were on the outside of the train walking through the train. We didn't know what they were doing, but in the morning we found out that they were looting these sacks that these girls had. They cut a window open on the sack and removed everything that was in it. These girls next morning had nothing. It was sort of bittersweet. I mean, we really didn't mean to be mean, but they were not very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived early in the morning and at the railroad station there were some of the Jews who were in hiding in Budapest waiting for these trains. They organized and they were waiting every train that came into Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the American Joint active at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Had nothing to do with it. Not at that time. At least, we didn't have anything to do with them. And so these were individuals--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what about UNRRA? Was UNRRA--

speaker: interviewee
text: Later. Later in Czechoslovakia UNRRA was very active. I don't know what happened in Hungary because we weren't there too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I recognized one of the boys from my hometown. And he immediately recognized me and embraced and he was happy to see somebody from his hometown coming home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took us into the school. And the walls of the school were covered with paper and alphabetically everybody signed in. So I went through the names hoping to find somebody from the family, at least a relative, an uncle, an aunt, a cousin. Nothing. Found absolutely nothing. Was getting late, was late in the afternoon. I started to cry and he kept reassuring me tomorrow we'll come back. Don't worry. You find somebody. And as I walked out the door, I pushed the door open and the door didn't give. Somebody was behind the door. And so I step back to let the person come in and my brother was standing there. I found out that he escaped from the labor camp and became a partisan, a freedom fighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czechoslovakia or in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Slovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Tatry mountains. He was with a ski troop. And there were some Jews hiding in those mountains, so they were supplying them. And we started to talk. Probably the most interesting story that I found actually appalling was that he told me that when he was with the Slovakian partisans that the Slovak partisans were so anti-Semitic that the Jewish boys were afraid to reveal that they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I asked my brother how many Jews were in your unit, he said we don't know. It was an unspoken word. We knew of each other, those who were Jewish, but it was never revealed because he said that we would've been shot in the back. So that was pretty upsetting after all that, that even those who hated the Germans, hated us even more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went to a town called Kosice where my brother's girlfriend was. And the two of them stayed in Kosice while I went back to Uzhorod, to my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother's girlfriend one he had acquired during the war or was that from before? Where was she during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was hiding in Budapest with Gentile papers. And of course until my brother was in labor camp, they had contact but after that they didn't. She also became very ill during hiding time. And, as a matter of fact, if I recall the first time when I went back home, she came with me. And I remember the Russian commander was in our home. And I walked in and I told him that I'm the [NON-ENGLISH] and that I owned the home. And they said, oh, that's all right. Pick a room. They would let me stay in a room if I want to stay. And then I realized that that's not the way l want to live and I'm not going to be there. And also our housekeeper, or old housekeeper lived upstairs in a little apartment. And she pretended that she was delighted that we came back. Of course, I'm sure she was worried that I'm going to ask back for the things that she probably took from our home, which I had no intentions doing. I didn't want anything from there. And she asked me what should she make for us to eat and I said to her take two dozen eggs and make dumplings out of them and then take two more dozen eggs and beat it like scrambled eggs over it. And my sister-in-law-- now she's my sister-in-law, my brother's girlfriend-- the two of us sat down and ate the dumplings with the scrambled eggs, which was a big pot. And then we went back. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you only took a few mementos from the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a little package where I found, as I said before, my father's gold watch. And my brother's bar mitzvah watch and my gold bracelet that my father bought me for my 18th birthday. Then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- we went back and we found out accidentally that my father-- somebody saw my father in Theresienstadt in a hospital. So we decided to go back to Prague and go to Terezin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We needed-- we found out that there may be a chance that it's him. And so my husband was look-- my brother was looking for a truck that if we find him we should be able to bring him from Terezin to Prague, to a hospital. And again accidentally we ran into my husband, Bernard, who was in the forced labor camp with my brother earlier. And they embraced and were very pleased to see each other. And my husband, he had access to an UNRRA truck. And they went to Theresienstadt and went into a room where my father's name was on the door. And my brother went from bed to bed searching for my father and couldn't find him. And then he came back again, looked at the list and sure enough my father's name was there. So he went back again and again he couldn't find him. And as he was walking out somebody called his name. And he turned back and he said he faced this skeleton of a man [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] who was dying of tuberculosis. They brought him to Prague. We took them to a very famous tuberculosis sanatorium called [INAUDIBLE] and a few months later my father died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before he died, he told us a very interesting story. He asked my brother whether on a certain date whether he escaped from the labor camp. I mean, my brother told him that he escaped, and my father said was it on such and such day? And my brother said, yeah. How do you know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB My father said that he marked the date for himself because that night-- [CRYING] --he dreamt that he was in the woods and this black horse was running. My brother's hair was very dark and beautiful black eyes. And he says I grabbed this horse and the horse wiggled himself out of my way and ran. And when I woke up next morning, he said, I knew that my son escaped. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After my father passed away, we both decided that we don't want to stay in Europe. And in the meantime, Bernard, my husband, kept coming, visiting after that. And when my father died, he told me that he promised my father that he's going to take care of me so he has to marry me. [LAUGHS] And so we got married. And a year later my sister-in-law had her first child and then the following year I had a child. And then we decided that we're going to leave Prague. Bernard, my husband, had papers to come to the United States because he lived in Prague in 1939 as a student and he had a student visa to come to the United States to a sister. And he was caught in Holland by the Nazis by the Gestapo and was brought back and went through the whole concentration camp experience. And so the American government acknowledged his papers and so we were put on the quota. But my brother couldn't so my brother went to France. And in France the Haganah got a hold of him and they went to Palestine, which is now the state of Israel. And even though we were lucky to find each other, we were again separated for 15 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 15 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do--

speaker: interviewee
text: After that when I finally was able to obtain papers, affidavits for them, then they came out here. And now luckily, fortunately, we are together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in the States? In America, where did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived to Allentown, Pennsylvania. It's very interesting. I want you to know that I'm going next week to Prague to my father's gravesite where I'm going to meet my brother who's going to be coming back from Israel where he is visiting now. And I'm going to go from Prague to Israel, so we will meet in the middle. And this is the first time that we're going to be back together and visit my father's gravesite on which, by the way, before we left we engraved my mother's and my sister's name since they didn't have a grave. And I came to Allentown, Pennsylvania, on the 31st of October, 1948. And it will be on 31st of October that I'm going to be returning this time from Israel. And it was not planned. I just when I saw it on paper, I realized. And I remember my brother-in-law waited for us at the airport. And I arrived-- I came with my daughter who was then 11 months old. My husband stayed back. He got his visa a little bit later and he wanted to go to England where he was a young brother in an orphanage. He was taken by the UNRRA by the way to an orphanage in England after the war. And the plane landed in-- in New York in the LaGuardia-- we arrived at the LaGuardia Airport. And everybody was getting off the plane and the captain came over to me and he said, you can't get off. You have to wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, why? I said, they're going to keep me here? What's the problem? I thought maybe something with the child. They are going to send me back. I was terribly frightened.

speaker: interviewee
text: When everybody was off the plane, then he said, well, we can go now. He took the child from me and walked me to the door of the stairs of the-- steps of the plane. Handed me the child and he said, now you can walk down. And downstairs some cameras were taking my picture and that's the picture I have on the mantle. I will show you later. When I arrived my brother-in-law apparently arranged it, some newspapers were there. I was one of the first refugees coming to Pennsylvania, to Allentown, and there was a big article about us. The interesting thing was that finally my brother-in-law was waiting for me at the airport and drove us to Allentown. And when I arrived to his home, he had two children. One was 10 years old, one was eight years old-- Michael and Susan. And they were dressed in costumes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my brother-in-law why are these children dressed in costumes? It is not Purim. And he said no. It's Halloween. And I said, what is Halloween? [LAUGHS] So this time when I come back, I know what Halloween is. Did you speak English when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke a little bit English. And my sister-in-law had a room arranged for us with a crib for the baby. She was-- by the way, my sister-in-law, my husband's half-sister who arranged for us to come to this country was not just a sister-in-law or a sister. She-- if I had my mother there waiting for me, she couldn't have been better, kinder to me, my sister Fanny. And we still are very, very close and her children are very close to us. And I remember my sister-in-law wanted me to take a bath and just go to bed and the baby should be in bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the two little children peaked through the door and they-- I waved to them to come in and they didn't. And then I said, come on in. And when they heard me say in English come on in they jumped on my bed. And they said, you speak English? And I said, little. And then they asked me, who did you vote for? I said what is vote? It was I think a day after or before that Truman was voted into-- President Truman was voted into office. So I remember these little incidents very fondly. And how was life after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Bernard arrived Christmas night with the We went to New York to wait for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you come together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't. We couldn't come together because it wasn't so simple. You know, Czechoslovakia by then was already under communist regime and it was just not possible for both of us to come at the same time. And on top of which, he wanted to go to England to visit this younger brother before he leaves Europe. We didn't know when he's going to be able to go back and see him or whether he's going to be able to come here. And we waited for the and everybody was brought down by stretchers. It was a terrible winter and stormy sea, I understand, all the way here. And my husband walked down eight pounds heavier than when I left him. He says on the boat he was the only one who ate [LAUGHS] and he gained a lot of weight. And a few weeks later, I-- in the meantime I was in touch with my aunt here in California. I had my father's sister here who came long before the war. And I was in touch with her and she insisted that we come to Los Angeles. And so, again, the Joint helped us to come out here by train. And I arrived on April 15, 1949. It was 115 degrees. It was a very hot day. And I looked at the palm trees and I said to my husband, I will never leave this town again. And I love Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in business here [INAUDIBLE] fashion designer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yes. Yes. I opened my own business very early. I didn't want to work for anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband work with you in this business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My husband was a schoolteacher in Europe, but he learned a trade here. And we were in business for 37 years. I had my own factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I became a well-known name in the industry. And I lived a very glamorous life as a fashion designer until one day I had a phone call from the Simon Wiesenthal Center asking me would I come to tell my story. And I laughed at Rabbi Cooper and I said, come on. After all these years why should I now start talking about those terrible days, those terrible weeks and years?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he proceeded to tell me that that night a Jewish cemetery was desecrated in the valley and-- and a temple was spray-painted with swastikas. And when I heard the word swastika I went crazy and I hung up on him. I said I have to think about it. And that night I was back in camp all night in my nightmare. Next morning, I woke up and I called him back and I said I'm ready to talk. I want you to understand that when we came here and I started my business and I realized that I have to really concentrate on a family, that we were a very unique little group of people from the age of 15 to the age of 40 maybe that we had no children and no elders. That we have to recreate a new nation. So that's what we concentrated on and that's what I did. And I didn't talk about the Holocaust, not even to my own child. Until she came later and asked me. And then of course she got very much involved in the subject and when she was I think 19 years old or so, she organized the first second generation group in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your only daughter, Klara?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my only daughter. She has a daughter, Johanna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her-- Johanna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was named after my mother. And she's now in Israel at Bar-Ilan University becoming religious, which I never dreamt will happen. But we are delighted about it. And myself got involved at the time with the Simon Wiesenthal Center and I became a public speaker for the Simon Wiesenthal Center. Soon after that, I had a conflict with the fashion industry and me talking about the Holocaust and I quit my business. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say with the--
